policy_statement: Enact a production tax credit for companies that turn recycled materials into intermediate products if they locate facilities in New York.
mechanism_description: The State should enact a production tax credit that encourages companies to convert recycled materials into intermediate products, increasing circular material use and reducing emissions associated with virgin material production.
primary_causal_pathway: Industrial production choices → ↑ recycled feedstock use → ↓ virgin material production → ↓ industrial energy + process emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Manufacturers and processors shift investment and operations toward converting recycled materials into intermediate industrial products (e.g., recycled polymers/pellets, recycled metals billet, recycled glass cullet) due to a production tax credit or similar incentive.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased availability and use of recycled intermediates substitutes for primary (virgin) material supply, avoiding upstream extraction/refining and reducing energy-intensive processing steps; this lowers combustion-related energy emissions and, in some sectors, direct process emissions associated with primary production.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle industrial GHG emissions (CO₂e), primarily through avoided virgin production and associated energy use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced industrial GHG emissions from avoided virgin material extraction and production due to displacement by recycled-material intermediates.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core causal chain is “incentivize recycled-material processing → displace virgin material production → reduce industrial energy/process emissions → reduce GHGs,” which is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although the policy promotes circularity/resource efficiency in an economic sense, the dominant climate effect is emissions reduction from industrial production; therefore it classifies as Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: If recycled processing is powered by high-carbon energy or induces additional production (rebound), benefits could shrink, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains emissions avoidance via virgin displacement.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy fitting this mechanism (incentivizing recycled-material intermediates specifically to substitute for virgin materials) shares the same primary mitigation pathway of lowering industrial emissions through displaced primary production.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More specific subtype details (e.g., which material stream—metals, plastics, cementitious materials—and whether benefits are mostly energy vs. process emissions) require policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible edge cases include: (a) electricity/heat for recycling supplied by carbon-intensive energy, reducing net gains; (b) limited displacement if recycled output does not substitute for virgin (quality/spec constraints), leading to smaller mitigation; (c) rebound effects if lower material costs increase total production; (d) some benefits may be economic development rather than climate, but the dominant intended climate pathway remains emissions reduction via virgin displacement.
key_indicators: "enact a production tax credit"; "applied to companies that turn recycled materials into intermediate products"; "if they locate their facilities in New York"
co_benefits: Economic development/job creation via in-state facility siting; reduced landfill disposal and associated local pollution
instance_edge_case_notes: Although structured as an economic development/siting incentive (location شرط), the core instrument is still a production tax credit (subsidy) aimed at changing industrial material inputs and thus emissions; no material ambiguity.
